title: Genmab — Hematology — Synthesis
html:
Genmab Hematology Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-24 Studies analyzed: EPKINLY DLBCL Promotional Effectiveness Tracker Q4 2023 (Post-Launch Wave 2), EPKINLY Heme MSL Impact Assessment Wave 1 (June-July 2025) Data sources: ZoomRx SharePoint deliverables + Genmab/AbbVie earnings and press releases

1. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: EPKINLY's Sales Force Is Competitive but MSLs Are the Real Differentiator

PET Q4 2023: EPKINLY and COLUMVI were tied in personal promotion reach (38% each), with POLIVY dominating at 56%. EPKINLY's overall call quality (75%) was competitive with COLUMVI (76%) but below MONJUVI (77%). Likelihood to increase prescription improved to 67% — second only to YESCARTA (81%) and POLIVY (68%).

MSL Wave 1 (2025): AbbVie/Genmab MSLs discussing epcoritamab achieved the leading share of voice with significantly higher reach vs most competitors, driven by community hospital reach. MSL effectiveness average was 74% (on par with glofitamab at 75%, ahead of mosunetuzumab at 66%). AbbVie/Genmab MSLs were rated most often as "Best in Class" and drove the strongest improvement in manufacturer perceptions.

What this means: The sales rep channel is competitive but not differentiated — EPKINLY is one voice among many in a crowded DLBCL promotional landscape. The MSL channel, however, is where AbbVie/Genmab has a measurable edge. The MSL "Best in Class" perception and ~60% high-impact interaction rate are distinctive. This suggests Genmab's commercial strategy should lean MORE on MSL-driven scientific exchange and LESS on traditional rep detailing for differentiation. ZoomRx's value proposition to Genmab should emphasize MSL ROI measurement.

Pattern 2: Messaging Effectiveness Diverges by Indication — FL Is Stronger Than DLBCL

PET Q4 2023 (DLBCL messaging): EPKINLY's most recalled message was the CAR-T exposed ORR (52% ORR, E10). The SubQ administration message (E15) was perceived as most motivating. Safety/CRS message (E14) was the most effective overall. But message recall broadly declined QoQ, with average correct association also declining — suggesting message fatigue in DLBCL.

MSL Wave 1 (DLBCL narratives): LTFU message (EP2: median DoCR 36.1 months with 3-year follow-up) was recalled most often and found most effective. epcor + GemOx combo data (EP3: 85% ORR, 61% CR in 2L+) was the second most effective but had lower recall.

MSL Wave 1 (FL narratives): FL narratives had HIGHER recall than DLBCL narratives (67% avg vs 47% avg). The safety message (EP7: manageable CRS, outpatient setting, no ICANS) was recalled most in FL. The 25-month follow-up combo data (EP8: ORR/CR 96%/87%) was the most effective FL narrative.

What this means: FL messaging is landing better than DLBCL messaging — higher recall, stronger effectiveness. This aligns with the FL combo data being genuinely more compelling (96% ORR vs 63% ORR). The DLBCL messaging challenge is that epcoritamab's monotherapy data (ORR 63%, CR 39%) is good but not dramatically superior to competitors (COLUMVI ORR 56%, CR 43% with fixed duration). In FL, the combo data is clearly differentiated. Genmab's messaging investment should prioritize FL.

Pattern 3: Community Adoption Is the Growth Vector — and MSL Reach Is Already There

PET Q4 2023: No explicit community vs academic segmentation in the PET data, but archetype analysis showed COLUMVI edged out EPKINLY in share of voice among "Enabled" HCPs (the most experienced/engaged segment), while EPKINLY was competitive among "Capable" HCPs (mid-tier, likely including more community prescribers).

MSL Wave 1: Epcoritamab MSL reach was significantly higher vs most competitors, specifically driven by higher reach among Community Hospital HCPs. AbbVie/Genmab manufacturer perceptions were notably stronger among Academic and Community HCPs vs Community Hospital HCPs — suggesting a perception gap in the community hospital segment that needs attention.

What this means: The MSL team is reaching community HCPs, but converting that reach into positive manufacturer perception in community hospitals is lagging behind academic and non-hospital community settings. This may reflect the step-up dosing complexity barrier — community hospital HCPs may be less comfortable managing CRS than academic or large community oncology practices. The implication: community hospital HCPs need more safety/practical administration education from MSLs, not more efficacy data.

Pattern 4: The CAR-T Exposed Narrative Is Epcoritamab's Unique Strategic Weapon

PET Q4 2023: The "CAR-T exposed ORR 52%" message (E10) was the most recalled EPKINLY message. It directly addresses the post-CAR-T treatment gap — a growing patient population as CAR-T use increases in 2L.

MSL Wave 1: In high-impact MSL interactions, both efficacy and clinical trial discussions were top topics recalled. The CAR-T subgroup data (EP4: ORR/CR 52%/35% in CAR-T exposed, 42%/28% in CAR-T refractory) was the third most effective DLBCL narrative.

What this means: No other bispecific has as robust a dataset in CAR-T exposed patients. As CAR-T use grows (Yescarta and Breyanzi both growing), more patients will eventually relapse post-CAR-T and need a next line of therapy. Epcoritamab's post-CAR-T data positions it as the natural sequencing partner after CAR-T failure. This is a narrative that becomes MORE important over time as the CAR-T-exposed patient pool grows.

2. Thesis Check

Thesis Status Evidence
1. FL combo is the real growth driver STRENGTHENED FL messaging more effective than DLBCL; NCCN Category 1; $468M FY2025 revenue with Q4 FL boost
2. 2L DLBCL positioning is vulnerable CONFIRMED EPCORE DLBCL-1 OS miss (HR 0.96); STARGLO OS positive (HR 0.59); PET data shows YESCARTA still dominant in prescribing intent
3. Bispecific vs CAR-T favors bispecifics in community EARLY SIGNAL MSL reach significantly higher in community hospitals; but manufacturer perception lags in community hospital segment
4. MSL effectiveness is a competitive advantage CONFIRMED "Best in Class" ratings; 74% effectiveness; ~60% high-impact interactions; stronger post-interaction research behavior
5. 1L DLBCL (EPCORE DLBCL-2) is franchise-defining PENDING Phase 1/2 data strong (98% ORR, 85% CR); Phase 3 readout expected 2026

3. Contradictions & Surprises

  1. EPKINLY's SubQ advantage isn't translating to clear promotional superiority in DLBCL. Despite being the only SubQ bispecific, EPKINLY is tied with COLUMVI in rep reach (38% each) and trails POLIVY (56%) and MONJUVI (49%) in the PET tracker. The SubQ convenience story resonates in MSL scientific exchange (E15 was the most motivating message) but isn't creating a decisive sales force advantage. This may be because the SubQ benefit is most relevant in the community setting, while the PET sample may over-index to academic/large practices.

  2. AbbVie/Genmab MSLs improve FL knowledge MORE than DLBCL knowledge. This is surprising given epcoritamab launched in DLBCL first and FL approval came later. It suggests the FL narrative is newer and therefore more "information-additive" to HCPs — whereas DLBCL HCPs have already absorbed the core epcoritamab data. Alternatively, it reflects stronger FL data that genuinely shifts physician understanding.

  3. BMS MSLs lead in "trusted partner" perception despite having the weakest product portfolio in the space. BMS has Breyanzi (CAR-T) and lenalidomide (the R2 backbone) but no bispecific. Yet their MSLs are perceived as more "trusted" than AbbVie/Genmab or Roche/Genentech. This suggests relationship depth and non-promotional credibility matter independently of product portfolio breadth. For Genmab, the implication is that "Best in Class" in message delivery does not automatically equal "most trusted" — the trust gap needs separate cultivation.

  4. EPKINLY's prescribing intent is driven by Capable HCPs, not Enabled HCPs. In the PET tracker, Enabled HCPs (the most experienced) showed declining call quality perceptions for EPKINLY, while Capable HCPs drove the increase in prescribing intent. This inverts expectations — the HCPs who should be the earliest adopters (Enabled) are showing fatigue, while mid-tier HCPs are warming up. This is actually good news for the community adoption thesis, since Capable HCPs are more likely to be in community settings.

4. Missing Data & Blind Spots

  1. No FL-specific promotional effectiveness data. The PET tracker (Q4 2023) is entirely DLBCL-focused, but FL is now the growth indication. ZoomRx should propose an FL PET or combined DLBCL/FL tracker.

  2. No post-FL-approval MSL data. The MSL Wave 1 was fielded June-July 2025, before the November 2025 FL combo approval. The FL narrative effectiveness data is pre-approval — post-approval dynamics could shift significantly.

  3. No real-world prescribing data linked to promotional metrics. Both the PET and MSL studies measure attitudes and perceptions, not actual prescribing behavior. Linking promotional effectiveness to script data would be the highest-value analytical step.

  4. No Genmab-specific revenue breakdown by indication. Genmab reports total Epkinly revenue ($468M FY2025) but does not publicly disclose DLBCL vs FL contribution. Understanding the revenue mix is essential for strategic prioritization.

  5. The PET tracker is from Q4 2023 — it is 2+ years old. The competitive landscape has shifted significantly since then (STARGLO approval, FL combo approval, EPCORE DLBCL-1 readout). Newer PET waves are needed to assess current promotional dynamics.

5. Implications for ZoomRx

Study Design Recommendations

  1. Launch an FL-specific bispecific tracker — measuring epcoritamab + R2 adoption vs mosunetuzumab monotherapy vs Breyanzi (CAR-T) in 2L+ FL. This is the fastest-growing segment.

  2. Segment all DLBCL tracking by practice setting — academic vs community vs community hospital. The MSL data shows dramatically different reach and perception patterns by setting.

  3. Design a post-CAR-T treatment pattern study — epcoritamab's post-CAR-T data is its unique competitive weapon. Track what happens to patients after CAR-T failure/relapse: who gets bispecific, who gets chemo, who gets clinical trial?

  4. Propose MSL ROI linkage study — connect MSL interaction quality scores to downstream prescribing changes for the same HCPs. This is the analytical gap that would make the MSL study 10x more valuable.

BD Conversation Starters

  1. "Your FL combo data is your strongest asset, but the MSL narratives for FL outperform DLBCL. Are you resourcing FL promotion proportionally? Our data can help optimize the DLBCL-vs-FL resource allocation."

  2. "Your MSLs are rated 'Best in Class' — but BMS is more trusted. Trust takes time; quality can be measured now. Let's build the longitudinal MSL-to-prescribing link that proves your MSL investment is driving Rx."

  3. "Community hospital MSL reach is your edge, but manufacturer perceptions lag there. That's a solvable problem — it likely means community hospital HCPs need more safety/administration education, not more efficacy messaging."

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
